text:
From the Language Design centric point-of-view, of the top of my mind. (With all those sources, the reader will have to do almost all the work themselves, but still, this is good stuff!)
• <https://books.google.se/books/about/Cognitive_Grammar.html?id=UKVNKz0ZRqwC&redir_esc=y|Cognitive Grammar> by Ronald Langacker (far too wordy introduction and still far away from readily applicable stuff, but this is as close as current linguistics get to practical PLD usefulness imo)
• For the language designer, learning the Language Esperanto fluently imo offers an intuitive feel/exploration tool for human grammar that not much else will
• All of <https://github.com/matthiasn/talk-transcripts|Rich Hickeys talks/transcripts> will reduce fear of transforming deep philosophy into practical language design
• <https://www.amazon.com/Constructing-User-Interface-Statecharts-Horrocks/dp/0201342782|Constructing the User Interface with Statecharts> (yes, there is a free pdf somewhere on the web!) this is a mind-opener in a very practical sense
• David Harel’s original statechart paper and especially his paper <https://www.wisdom.weizmann.ac.il/~harel/SCANNED.PAPERS/VisualFormalisms.pdf|On Visual Formalisms> (avoid all papers about how to merge statecharts with OO, as that added some tooling for handling complexity in OO, but detracted from imo more fruitful directions such as generalizing (like letting them mingle with ADT’s :slightly_smiling_face: ) and in general adapting statecharts for PLD needs)
I quite like historical papers and books, not so much for what they say about the future but for what they convey about the mindset of invention, here are some:
+ Andrew Hodges <https://www.turing.org.uk/book/|Alan Turing biography> (the book; avoid the film at all costs)
+ Rich Hickeys <https://clojure.org/about/history|HOPL paper>
+ David Harel’s <https://www.wisdom.weizmann.ac.il/~harel/papers/Statecharts.History.pdf|Statecharts in the Making: A Personal Account>
etc.

I quite like historical papers and books, not so much for what they say about the future but for what they convey about the mindset of invention, here are some:
+ Andrew Hodges
Alan Turing biography

+ Rich Hickeys
HOPL paper
+ David Harel’s
Statecharts in the Making: A Personal Account
etc.

text: Thanks for sharing, that _is_ a great podcast, just the mindset of the people make worth listening to and then lots of interesting stuff!

elements.text: Thanks for sharing, that
is
a great podcast, just the mindset of the people make worth listening to and then lots of interesting stuff!

text: Computer Lib/Dream Machines by Ted Nelson is my personal favorite! I'll also recommend The Art of Game Design by Jesse Schell which is a neat way to view designing systems that lead users to achieve challenging goals (programming languages can definitely be seen through that lens) while keeping things fun.

I'll also recommend The Art of Game Design by Jesse Schell which is a neat way to view designing systems that lead users to achieve challenging goals (programming languages can definitely be seen through that lens) while keeping things fun.

attachments.text:
Mindstorms: Children, Computers, and Powerful Ideas is a book by computer scientist Seymour Papert, in which he argues for the benefits of teaching computer literacy in primary and secondary education. It was published by Basic Books in 1980, and republished in a new edition by Basic Books in 1993.
The Lego Mindstorms programmable construction set system is named after the <http://book.In|book.In> 2017, thanks to Papert's family the book was made freely available online here.
Papert describes the Turtle as an "object-to-think-with" and discusses many code examples of Turtle Graphics.
attachments.title: Mindstorms (book)
